M5: Two-level industries and calibration

JoonHo Lee

2026-06-06

Source: vignettes/m5-two-level-and-calibration.Rmd
m5-two-level-and-calibration.Rmd

Abstract

This is the last method-track vignette, and it has two parts. The first extends the one-level grading to industries. The industry model splits each firm’s standardized effect into a within-industry component and a between-industry component, deconvolves the two as a tensor-product prior, and pushes that prior forward to the firm scale with gradepath’s native theta-pushforward. Two firms in the same industry share one between-industry effect, so it cancels in their comparison and they are compared on the within-industry component alone. The industry pipeline grades a firm-level route and a between-industry route and assembles two report cards. We report its status honestly as PARTIAL_ACCEPTED: the exact industry grade counts pass, but the continuous discordance, agreement, and fit rows are classified by fixture parity, not by direct reproduction of the paper’s industry values, and we read that ledger live. The second part covers gp_calibrate, the seeded Monte-Carlo that checks the grading method’s frequentist guarantees on a fitted model, and explains why KRW’s Table E1 study is deliberately deferred.

This is the fifth and final method-track vignette. The first four built and verified the one-level pipeline end to end: m1 fixed the notation, m2 derived the precision exponent and the r-scale, m3 built the prior, the posteriors, and the pairwise matrix Π\Pi, and m4 turned Π\Pi into grades along the information-reliability frontier. This vignette closes the track with two extensions that sit around that core rather than inside it.

The first half is the two-level industry model — the same grading machinery, but applied to firms nested in industries, with a between-industry effect that every firm in an industry shares. The second half is calibration — a seeded Monte-Carlo, gp_calibrate(), that checks whether the grading method’s frequentist guarantees survive estimating the prior. Both halves summarize the theory and cite Kline, Rose & Walters (Kline et al. 2024); the formal two-level statements are in their Online Appendix and the Monte-Carlo design is their Table E1 study.

Two disciplines run through the vignette. Nothing solves here: the two-level grade solve and gp_calibrate() each run an integer program (the calibration runs one per draw), so they appear only as eval = FALSE illustrations, and the one live panel reads the M2 acceptance ledger, a fast lookup. And the honesty discipline of the method track holds: the industry surface is reported exactly as PARTIAL_ACCEPTED, never inflated, and gp_calibrate() is named for what it is — a qualitative diagnostic, not a reproduction of any published number.

Part I — The two-level industry model

1. From one level to two: why industries

The one-level model of m1–m4 grades nn firms against one prior GG over a single latent quantity. KRW’s industry analysis asks a structured question on top of that: how much of a firm’s measured discrimination is the firm, and how much is its industry? A staffing firm and a bank may both post a wide White-minus-Black contact-rate gap, but if staffing as a whole runs wide while this particular bank is wide for a bank, those two firms are extreme for different reasons. The one-level model cannot see that distinction — it has one latent effect per firm and no notion of the group the firm sits in.

The two-level model adds exactly that notion. It writes each firm’s standardized effect as the combination of a within-industry component, specific to the firm, and a between-industry component, shared by every firm in the same industry. With the effect decomposed this way, gradepath can grade firms within the structure (the firm-level route) and grade the industries themselves (the between-industry route), producing two report cards instead of one. Everything downstream — the pairwise matrix, the grade integer program, the frontier — is the same machinery m4 already derived; only the prior and the posterior that feed it change. This is Milestone M2, deferred behind the one-level M1 of the earlier vignettes, and the honesty section (§5) is about exactly how far that milestone got.

2. The two-level model: within, between, and the pushforward

We summarize the model; the full derivation is the package’s design (its two-level derivation), and the formal statements are KRW (Kline et al. 2024). Write k(i)k(i) for the industry of firm ii. The two-level decomposition of the firm’s precision-independent effect viv_i has a within-industry component ξi\xi_i (one per firm, drawn from a within-industry distribution GξG_\xi) and a between-industry component ηk(i)\eta_{k(i)} (one per industry, drawn from a between-industry distribution GηG_\eta and shared by every firm in industry kk). For the race panel the two combine multiplicatively,

vi=ηk(i)ξi,θi=siβηk(i)ξi, v_i \;=\; \eta_{k(i)}\,\xi_i, \qquad \theta_i \;=\; s_i^{\beta}\,\eta_{k(i)}\,\xi_i,

while for the gender panel they combine additively, vi=ηk(i)+ξiv_i = \eta_{k(i)} + \xi_i, with θi=μ+siβ(ηk(i)+ξi)\theta_i = \mu + s_i^{\beta}(\eta_{k(i)} + \xi_i). The familiar siβs_i^{\beta} rescaling — the precision dependence of m2 — is unchanged; what is new is the split of the latent effect into a firm part and an industry part.

The tensor-product prior. The two components are deconvolved jointly into the tensor product

G=Gξ⊗Gη, G \;=\; G_\xi \otimes G_\eta,

a two-dimensional distribution on the (ξ,η)(\xi, \eta) grid: GξG_\xi on the within-industry support, GηG_\eta on the between-industry support. gradepath fits both at once by a penalized log-spline maximum likelihood — the native two-level deconvolution, which reuses the one-level spline primitives but is not the one-level engine, since the joint fit, its cluster-robust GMM moments, and its support caps have no one-level analogue. A within-share constraint pins the between component to the multiplicative or additive identity — E[η]=1E[\eta] = 1 for race, E[η]=0E[\eta] = 0 for gender — so the industry layer carries only the shared departure from the average firm, and the within component carries the rest.

The theta-pushforward. The prior lives on the (ξ,η)(\xi, \eta) scale, but the grading reads a prior on the firm θ\theta-scale. The bridge is an internal theta-pushforward step (gradepath’s gp_pushforward_theta, an internal kernel mirroring KRW’s get_g_theta.m; the two-level pipeline runs it for you): it marginalizes the joint prior over both supports, forming θ=sβξη\theta = s^{\beta}\,\xi\,\eta (race) or θ=μ+sβ(ξ+η)\theta = \mu + s^{\beta}(\xi + \eta) (gender) on a fixed grid and accumulating the joint mass gξ⋅gηg_\xi \cdot g_\eta at each θ\theta value. The one-level pushforward of m3 takes a single support and cannot do this — it has the wrong arity for two dimensions — so the two-level pushforward is a separate native kernel. The output is an ordinary θ\theta-scale prior, and from there the posterior, the pairwise matrix, and the grade integer program are exactly the m3–m4 objects.

3. The same-industry override

The single most consequential — and most error-prone — line in the two-level model is what happens when a pairwise comparison falls within an industry. The posterior compares two firms by integrating an ordering indicator against the firms’ likelihood weights. When firm ii and firm jj sit in different industries, their industry effects ηk(i)\eta_{k(i)} and ηk(j)\eta_{k(j)} are independent draws, so the joint weight genuinely factorizes into the product of the two firms’ likelihoods,

Lij=Li⋅Lj(k(i)≠k(j)). L_{ij} \;=\; L_i \cdot L_j \qquad (k(i) \neq k(j)).

When firm ii and firm jj sit in the same industry, they share the one between-industry effect ηk\eta_k. The likelihood evidence for that shared effect has been observed once, not twice, so the joint weight must condition on the single ηk\eta_k and the likelihood enters once:

Lij=Li(=Lj)(k(i)=k(j)). L_{ij} \;=\; L_i \;\bigl(= L_j\bigr) \qquad (k(i) = k(j)).

This is the same-industry override: within an industry the between-industry component is common to both firms, so it cancels in their comparison, and the within-industry component ξ\xi alone governs which firm is more extreme. Two banks both inherit whatever the banking industry’s effect is, so that shared inheritance says nothing about which bank has the wider contact-rate gap — only their firm-specific ξ\xi does.

Forming the product Li⋅Lj=Li2L_i \cdot L_j = L_i^2 for a same-industry pair would instead square the industry’s evidence, double-counting the one shared ηk\eta_k as though it had been seen twice and biasing every within-industry comparison. The override is scoped strictly to the two-level case — in the one-level model there is no shared industry effect, and it must not fire — and it is social-choice plumbing in the pairwise construction, not an empirical-Bayes step, so it propagates to every pairwise matrix the two-level posterior produces.

4. The two-level pipeline

The industry analysis is three verbs, all of which need constructed two-level inputs and a solve, so they are shown but not run here. The pipeline mirrors the one-level path of m4 but carries two routes side by side: a firm-level route (industry_rfe) graded from the firm Πθ\Pi_\theta, and a between-industry route (btwn) graded from the industry-level Π\Pi over the s‾η\bar s\,\eta orderings.

Build the two-level pairwise matrices. gp_twolevel_pairwise() takes the firm-level and between-industry pairwise inputs (with the same-industry override already encoded in their integrals) and bundles them for the two routes:

# Builds the two-level outranking bundle for the two routes.
# Needs constructed two-level inputs -- shown, not run.
pw <- gp_twolevel_pairwise(Pi_theta, Pi_bar,
                           ids             = firm_ids,
                           industry_levels = industry_ids,
                           control         = gp_control(backend = "gurobi"))

Solve the two-level grades. gp_twolevel_grade() routes each bundle into the package grade solver — one grade path per route, read off at the reporting λ\lambda. It solves, so it is illustrative only:

# Solves one grade integer program per route -- shown, not run.
tlg <- gp_twolevel_grade(pw,
                         posterior = twolevel_posterior,  # backs the report cards
                         lambda    = 0.25)                 # the reporting penalty

The returned gp_twolevel_grade carries an industry_rfe slot (the firm-level route: its grade path, selected grade, grade count, and — when a posterior is supplied — a firm report card) and a btwn slot (the same payload for the between-industry route), along with a combined producer_status that is "OK" only when both routes are acceptance-ready.

Select a report card. gp_twolevel_report_card() returns the posterior-backed card for the chosen route:

# The firm-level industry card, or the between-industry card -- shown, not run.
firm_card <- gp_twolevel_report_card(tlg, model = "industry_rfe")
ind_card  <- gp_twolevel_report_card(tlg, model = "btwn")

One scope note the next section makes precise: a report card exists only when the two-level grade was built with a posterior. While the industry surface is PARTIAL_ACCEPTED, the package exports no public builder for a posterior-backed two-level grade, so a grade assembled from bare Π\Pi matrices is structural only and carries no card — calling the selector on it raises an informative error that points back to the status ledger. The verbs are documented and shown, but the success path runs on internal fixtures, not on user-constructed input; that deliberate boundary is itself part of the honest status.

5. The honest M2 status: PARTIAL_ACCEPTED

Here is the one live panel of the vignette, and the heart of the honesty story. The two-level milestone did not reach full acceptance, and the package says so in a structured ledger you can read instantly — no solve, just a lookup. The public summary is gp_m2_status():

gp_m2_status()[, c("component", "status", "detail")]
#>                   component           status
#> 1                    formal PARTIAL_ACCEPTED
#> 2 L01_industry_grade_counts             PASS
#> 3       L02_race_continuous   APPROXIMATE_OK
#> 4     L02_gender_continuous         PROMOTED
#> 5        N10_support_guards      EVIDENCE_OK
#> 6        M1_status_boundary NOT_RECALCULATED
#>                                                              detail
#> 1                           ONE_OR_MORE_L02_ROWS_REMAIN_APPROXIMATE
#> 2                                        2/2 exact count rows pass.
#> 3   Race L02 remains approximate: Pi_theta gap 0.0121756787 > 0.01.
#> 4 Gender L02 promoted to banded: Pi_theta gap 0.0066335820 <= 0.01.
#> 5                     4/4 N10 rows carry synthetic evidence status.
#> 6       M1 acceptance status is not recalculated by gp_m2_status().

Read the six rows plainly. They tell a precise, un-inflated story:

  • formal -> PARTIAL_ACCEPTED. The overall verdict, with the reason "ONE_OR_MORE_L02_ROWS_REMAIN_APPROXIMATE". One or more continuous rows did not clear the parity band, so the milestone is partial — not accepted, not failed.
  • L01_industry_grade_counts -> PASS. The exact part. The industry grade counts are integer, optimizer-determined quantities, and they match the archive exactly ("2/2 exact count rows pass"). This is a genuine, registered, exact reproduction.
  • L02_race_continuous -> APPROXIMATE_OK. The race continuous summaries (discordance, agreement, fit) remain approximate: the recorded Πθ\Pi_\theta fixture gap is 0.0121756787, above the 0.01 promotion threshold. The status is honest — APPROXIMATE_OK means “documented as approximate,” not “passed.”
  • L02_gender_continuous -> PROMOTED. The gender continuous summaries were promoted to banded: the recorded gender Πθ\Pi_\theta fixture gap is 0.0066335820, at or below 0.01. Promotion means the gender panel’s fixture artifacts met the intermediate parity band used for M2 — it does not mean the paper’s industry discordance, agreement, or fit targets were reproduced directly. This is fixture-parity evidence, not paper-value reproduction.
  • N10_support_guards -> EVIDENCE_OK. Four synthetic support guards carry evidence ("4/4 N10 rows carry synthetic evidence status"). These are guards on the support construction, not registered paper-value passes.
  • M1_status_boundary -> NOT_RECALCULATED. The M2 ledger is M2-only. It reads the two-level surface and deliberately does not touch or recompute the one-level M1 acceptance contract.

The honest reading, stated once and plainly: M2 is PARTIAL_ACCEPTED. The exact industry grade counts pass (L01). The continuous discordance, agreement, and fit rows are classified by fixture parity — gender promoted to banded at gap 0.0066, race still approximate at gap 0.0122 > 0.01 — and a promotion is intermediate-band evidence, not direct reproduction of the paper’s industry values. The N10 rows are synthetic support guards, not paper-value passes. A PROMOTED row is never the same as an ACCEPTED row, and the package never lets it read as one.

The full ledger behind this one-screen summary is gp_m2_acceptance(), a gp_m2_acceptance object whose slots include table (the row-by-row gate ledger with paper and replicated values), checks (an alias of table), effective_registry (the registry after L02 promotion), fixture_gates (the race/gender fixture evidence used), and summary (the counts by status). The status verb above is the read; the acceptance object is the receipt.

Part II — Calibration

6. The KRW Monte-Carlo study (Table E1)

We now turn from the industry extension to a different kind of question: does the grading method’s statistical guarantee survive having to estimate the prior? KRW answer it with an Online-Appendix Monte-Carlo study — Table E1 — and gradepath ships a small stand-in for it. We summarize the study here and explain the stand-in next.

Table E1 is a calibration study, not a replication target. Its logic, summarized:

  • Known data-generating process, calibrated to the fit. The estimated prior GG and the precision-dependence exponent β\beta from the real deconvolution are treated as the simulation’s ground truth. Each draw samples firm-level random effects from that prior, builds true effects θi=siβvi\theta_i = s_i^{\beta} v_i with the observed standard errors sis_i held fixed, and adds normal measurement error θ̂i=θi+siεi\hat\theta_i = \theta_i + s_i\,\varepsilon_i.
  • Oracle versus empirical. Two grading rules run on every synthetic dataset. The oracle uses the true prior that generated the data — it never has to estimate anything. The empirical re-estimates the prior from the synthetic draw through the same pipeline a researcher would actually run. The gap between the two gradings is regret: the cost of not knowing the prior.
  • What it certifies. Small regret across draws means estimating the prior barely distorts the grades — credible-interval coverage and discordance-rate control hold up under prior-estimation uncertainty. This is a statement about the method’s frequentist properties, scoped to the race dimension.

In gradepath’s verification substrate these correspond to a block of Table-E1 rows that sit in the published-value registry but are explicitly outside the package’s headline scorecard. Only the rendered tableE1.tex ships; there is no machine-readable numeric artifact behind those rows. That fact drives the next section.

7. Why Table E1 is deferred (GP-DEC-18-A)

The deferral of Table E1 from the core replication suite is a deliberate, recorded design decision — GP-DEC-18-A. gradepath’s harness checks replicated quantities against a registry of published values with per-id tolerances. The E1 rows resist that machinery on three independent axes, each sufficient on its own:

  1. No parseable artifact to check against. The companion ships formatted LaTeX (tableE1.tex) and nothing machine-readable behind it; the companion itself excludes E1 from its own headline suite for exactly this reason. A check that cannot read its own ground truth is not a check.
  2. RNG parity is impossible by construction. The study’s posterior integration draws from a 10710^7-sample Matlab stream seeded at 15238. gradepath replaces simulation with deterministic quadrature and cannot reproduce that stream bit-for-bit, so any reproduction of E1 would be approximate by construction — it could match the qualitative conclusion but never the exact E1 cell to a tight band.
  3. It validates the method, not a headline number gradepath must hit. E1 certifies a property — that regret is small and that discordance-rate control survives prior estimation — not a published number the package is obligated to reproduce. Its cost-to-value for parity is low against the grade counts, distributions, and discordance / agreement / fit scalars that are in the headline suite.

So the full Table E1 Monte-Carlo stays outside the core replication suite, and the package ships a smaller object that honors the spirit of E1 — confirming the method’s guarantees hold — without pretending to a row-for-row fidelity it cannot deliver. Were E1 to be graduated into the suite some day, two artifacts not present today would be needed: a tableE1.tex parser that recovers its numeric cells into machine-readable truth, and a Monte-Carlo-aware tolerance class that compares against a simulation band rather than a point. Until both exist, E1 stays documented-but-not-checked, and the stand-in below stands in.

8. The gp_calibrate() stand-in

gp_calibrate() is that stand-in: a standalone, seeded, qualitative Monte-Carlo that runs on a fitted model. It treats the fitted prior as the truth, simulates n_sim synthetic datasets from it, re-runs the full one-level pipeline on each, and reports — as a compact gp_calibration object — whether the method’s own frequentist guarantees hold qualitatively. The signature:

# Runs one grade-IP solve PER DRAW -- shown, not run. Never runs on render.
fit <- gp_parity_fit("race")
cal <- gp_calibrate(fit,
                    n_sim     = 200L,    # synthetic datasets to draw and re-fit
                    seed      = 15238L,  # in-package reproducibility only
                    dr_target = 0.05,    # the discordance-rate target for dr_ok
                    ci_level  = 0.9,     # the nominal interval level for coverage_ok
                    min_ok    = 1L)      # minimum successful draws before reporting
cal
summary(cal)

The return gp_calibration carries the run scalars dr_mean (mean empirical discordance rate over successful draws), coverage (mean interval coverage), and regret_mean (mean regret of the empirical grading against the oracle), plus the two boolean verdicts dr_ok and coverage_ok. It prints as a compact, result-first one-liner and holds no posterior matrices and no per-draw archive.

How to read the three quantities:

  • dr_mean near dr_target with dr_ok = TRUE: the rule’s discordance-rate control survives re-estimating the prior.
  • coverage near ci_level with coverage_ok = TRUE: credible intervals cover the truth near nominal under prior-estimation uncertainty.
  • regret_mean small and non-negative: the empirical grading stays close to the oracle that knows the true prior.

Two scale subtleties, stated plainly, because they are what make the quantities mean what they say. First, the oracle grades the known true-prior pairwise without re-deconvolving — its pairwise comes straight from the true prior — and both gradings, oracle and empirical, are scored on that same true-prior pairwise. Because the oracle is by definition the risk-minimizer on that pairwise, the empirical grading can only match or fall short of it, so

regret≥0by construction. \text{regret} \;\ge\; 0 \qquad \text{by construction.}

Regret is never negative; a regret of zero means the re-estimated prior reproduced the oracle grading exactly. Second, coverage is assessed on the β\beta-invariant θ\theta-scale: the refit’s θ\theta-scale reporting interval is compared against the true θi=siβvi\theta_i = s_i^{\beta} v_i. Scoring on the θ\theta-scale keeps coverage coherent even when a refit’s estimated β\beta differs from the truth’s, which it generally does.

Small-fit honesty. The two verdicts are honest, not cosmetic. On a tiny or weakly identified fit the one-level β\beta-GMM is noisy and its standardized scale is ill-identified, so the verdicts are frequently FALSE and many synthetic refits are skipped and counted in n_failed (the reported means are over the successful draws only; if too few succeed the harness errors rather than averaging noise). Meaningful calibration needs a well-identified fit — KRW’s real multi-firm input. The harness is robust to the occasional non-convergent draw and reports honestly either way; it never tunes the data to force a pass.

To keep the boundary sharp, gp_calibrate() deliberately is not seeded to match Matlab’s 15238 stream (the shared default seed is a cosmetic homage; gradepath’s RNG and its quadrature substitute mean the synthetic data differ from the study by construction), not the full multi-model, multi-hundred-draw grid of the study (it runs a small n_sim on the already-fitted model), and not a member of any default scorecard, headline result, or reproduction of any Table E1 cell. It is a diagnostic. Because each draw re-solves a grade integer program, it never runs on package load or on vignette render — every calibration chunk here is eval = FALSE — and it prints a one-line budget heads-up before the loop when n_sim times the unit count exceeds 600, an informational message that never changes the result.

9. Where to next

That closes the method track. Over five vignettes the one-level pipeline was derived and verified end to end, the grading was extended to the two-level industry model and reported honestly as PARTIAL_ACCEPTED, and the method’s frequentist guarantees were given a seeded stand-in for the deferred Table E1 study. To revisit any stage:

  • Foundations and notation — m1 fixes the symbol table, the grade-direction triple, and the firms-vs-names welfare flip every later vignette relies on.
  • Precision and standardization — m2 derives the r-scale and the β\beta-GMM step, the same precision dependence the two-level model carries.
  • Deconvolution and posterior — m3 builds the one-level prior, the posteriors, and the pairwise matrix Π\Pi this vignette extends to two levels and re-uses under calibration.
  • Grading, the frontier, and report cards — m4 derives the grade integer program and the frontier that both the two-level routes and each calibration draw solve.

For the workflow side, the applied track runs the same pipeline hands-on: a1 is the first contact, a2 walks the grading stage with race and gender side by side, a3 covers the report-card and frontier accessors, a4 is the figure gallery, and a5 covers the solver backends and the calibration verbs in workflow terms. Throughout, the grade is a sorting by posterior evidence, never a contest: gradepath’s printed output is result-first and quantity-anchored (the package decision GP-DEC-14-A), so the same code path serves firms and names — and the two-level routes and the calibration draws — without the printed text inverting on one of them. The grade label is an integer in {1,…,n}\{1, \dots, n\}, a sorting index, not a letter grade.

Provenance 

sessionInfo()
#> R version 4.6.0 (2026-04-24)
#> Platform: aarch64-apple-darwin23
#> Running under: macOS Tahoe 26.2

References

Kline, Patrick, Evan K. Rose, and Christopher R. Walters. 2024. “A Discrimination Report Card.” American Economic Review 114 (8): 2472–525. https://doi.org/10.1257/aer.20230700.

On this page

Developed by JoonHo Lee.

Site built with pkgdown 2.2.0.
